title: TCP Output Connection Failures (_internal)
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd (TcpOutputProc OR group=tcpout_connections)
| search "connection" AND ("failed" OR "refused" OR "timed out" OR "broken pipe")
| stats count by host, destIp, destPort
| sort -count
description: Forwarders and intermediate tiers rely on TCP out to indexers. Connection failures mean data is queued or dropped at the source.
value: Forwarders and intermediate tiers rely on TCP out to indexers. Connection failures mean data is queued or dropped at the source.
implementation: Forward `splunkd` logs with TCP output connection events. Alert on rising failure counts per destination. Verify indexer reachability, certificates, and firewall paths.
visualization: Table (failures by destination), Line chart (failure rate), Status grid (forwarder × output group).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` (TcpOutputProc, `group=tcpout_connections`).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Forward `splunkd` logs with TCP output connection events. Alert on rising failure counts per destination. Verify indexer reachability, certificates, and firewall paths.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd (TcpOutputProc OR group=tcpout_connections)
| search "connection" AND ("failed" OR "refused" OR "timed out" OR "broken pipe")
| stats count by host, destIp, destPort
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**TCP Output Connection Failures (_internal)** — Forwarders and intermediate tiers rely on TCP out to indexers. Connection failures mean data is queued or dropped at the source.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` (TcpOutputProc, `group=tcpout_connections`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, destIp, destPort** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search over calm and busy windows. Compare with the Splunk monitoring console and relevant health and introspection views, and spot-check key fields in sample events.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (failures by destination), Line chart (failure rate), Status grid (forwarder × output group).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how we use and protect smart assistant and model calls so cost, quality, and misuse stay in a range we are willing to own.
knownFalsePositives: Signals move after deploys, config edits, and traffic shifts. We compare with the right product view and a short history before an outage page.
